meta.notes: Normalized hierarchy with consistent child naming. Added final Energy taxonomy with Energy Sector as a family parent, 6 core editorial layers, LNG as a Midstream sub-layer, and Energy overlays. Existing Nuclear Renaissance and Energy Transition baskets are preserved without duplicating Nuclear Renaissance. AI exposure overlay included at file end.

AI Infrastructure.description: The full physical backbone of AI compute, from power generation to the data center floor. Parent basket aggregating all AI infrastructure sub-layers.

AI Infra: Power Generation.description: Who produces the electricity for AI workloads: nuclear restarts, IPPs with data center PPAs, and next-generation nuclear.
AI Infra: Power Generation.article_narrative: Before a single GPU fires, someone has to generate the power. Data center electricity demand is rising fast, and nuclear plus dispatchable generation sit at the base of the AI capex cycle.

AI Infra: Electrical Equipment & Cooling.description: Transformers, switchgear, UPS, power distribution, and thermal management used to convert electricity into reliable AI compute capacity.
AI Infra: Electrical Equipment & Cooling.article_narrative: Once power reaches the data center fence, it must be stepped down, distributed, protected, and cooled. This is one of the key bottleneck layers in AI infrastructure.

AI Infra: Engineering & Construction.description: The contractors physically building AI factories, electrical interconnections, and mission-critical data center capacity.
AI Infra: Engineering & Construction.article_narrative: Someone has to build all of this. These firms benefit from record backlogs, electrical complexity, and skilled-labor scarcity.

AI Infra: Data Center Operators.description: The operators and owners of the physical buildings and sites that host AI compute, including colocation platforms and crypto-to-AI pivots.
AI Infra: Data Center Operators.article_narrative: This is the real estate and site-power layer of the AI boom. It includes established colocation leaders and newer platforms converting power-heavy facilities into AI/HPC hosting.

AI Infra: Networking & Optical.description: High-speed ethernet switches, optical transceivers, fiber, and connectors linking GPUs inside clusters and across campuses.
AI Infra: Networking & Optical.article_narrative: A GPU cluster is only as fast as its interconnect. This layer benefits from every incremental rack, cluster, and campus added to the AI buildout.

AI Infra: Server & Compute Hardware.description: Server OEMs, storage systems, and rack-level hardware that package GPUs into usable AI systems.
AI Infra: Server & Compute Hardware.article_narrative: This is the final physical layer before workloads are deployed: servers, storage, and system integration around accelerated compute.

AI Silicon.description: The complete chip supply chain feeding AI training and inference: accelerators, memory, equipment, foundries, and EDA tools.

AI Silicon: Compute & Accelerators.description: GPU and accelerator designers at the heart of AI training and inference.
AI Silicon: Compute & Accelerators.article_narrative: This is where the AI gold rush begins: the processors doing the training and inference work.

AI Silicon: Memory & Storage.description: HBM, NAND, and storage components that feed and retain AI datasets and model workflows.
AI Silicon: Memory & Storage.article_narrative: Memory is one of the quiet bottlenecks of the AI stack, especially high-bandwidth memory tied to advanced accelerators.

AI Silicon: Semiconductor Equipment.description: The tools and materials used to fabricate advanced semiconductors.
AI Silicon: Semiconductor Equipment.article_narrative: Upstream of every chip winner sit the equipment makers that enable the whole industry to build advanced nodes and packaging.

AI Silicon: Analog & Edge Chips.description: Analog, mixed-signal, power management, and edge inference semiconductors for on-device and industrial AI.
AI Silicon: Analog & Edge Chips.article_narrative: Not all AI runs in hyperscale data centers. As models spread to cars, factories, devices, and edge systems, these chips matter more.

AI Silicon: Foundries.description: The fabs that manufacture advanced AI chips and related silicon.
AI Silicon: Foundries.article_narrative: Design is one thing; manufacturing is another. This layer is the geopolitical and capacity chokepoint of the AI stack.

AI Silicon: EDA & Design Tools.description: Electronic design automation tools that enable AI chip design and verification.
AI Silicon: EDA & Design Tools.article_narrative: Before any accelerator can be fabricated, it must be designed and verified. EDA is a high-moat duopoly inside the silicon stack.

AI Cloud Platforms.description: Hyperscalers and AI-native cloud platforms monetizing compute, storage, and AI-as-a-service at scale.
AI Cloud Platforms.article_narrative: These are the biggest buyers and sellers of AI compute. Their capex drives demand through the rest of the stack while their platforms monetize enterprise AI adoption.

AI Cloud Platforms: Core.description: Core hyperscalers and AI-native cloud platforms monetizing compute, storage, and AI-as-a-service at scale.
AI Cloud Platforms: Core.article_narrative: These are the biggest buyers and sellers of AI compute. Their capex drives demand through the rest of the stack while their platforms monetize enterprise AI adoption.

AI Applications.description: The software and workflow layer where AI creates end-user value. Parent basket aggregating application-level AI monetizers.
sub_baskets: AI Apps: Vibe Coding & Dev Agents

AI Apps: Software & Workflow.description: Enterprise software, workflow, design, marketing, and automation platforms monetizing AI through copilots, agents, and productivity layers.
AI Apps: Software & Workflow.article_narrative: This is where AI becomes visible to users and enterprises through workflow automation, content creation, and task acceleration.

AI Apps: Cybersecurity.description: Security platforms using AI to detect, prioritize, automate, and defend against AI-native threats.
AI Apps: Cybersecurity.article_narrative: Cybersecurity is now an AI application category in its own right: AI defends against AI-generated threats while also improving analyst productivity and workflow automation.

AI Apps: Vertical AI.description: Companies applying AI to domain-specific workflows such as life sciences, clinical data, or scientific discovery.
AI Apps: Vertical AI.article_narrative: Vertical AI is where proprietary data and domain expertise meet model workflows, often creating higher switching costs than generic AI tools.

AI Apps: Vibe Coding & Dev Agents.description: An exotic AI theme focused on natural-language software creation, coding copilots, agentic development tools, and the second-order demand beneficiaries across cloud, databases, observability, and security.
AI Apps: Vibe Coding & Dev Agents.article_narrative: As AI makes software creation faster and more accessible, the number of experiments, apps, automations, and deployed agents rises. That expands demand not only for coding tools, but also for cloud runtime, data storage, observability, and AI-native security.

AI Disruption Risk.description: Business models structurally threatened by AI automation, especially weaker IT services, legacy software, and selected media models.
AI Disruption Risk.article_narrative: This basket captures the adaptation risk side of AI: the businesses that may face margin pressure, relevance decay, or workflow disintermediation as AI automates core tasks.

Asset Management & Alternatives.description: Alternative asset managers, traditional asset managers, and wealth platforms with AUM-driven business models and performance-fee optionality.

Capital Markets & Exchanges.description: Exchanges, market-data franchises, investment banks, and trading infrastructure.

Consumer Staples Resilience.description: Defensive consumer names across grocery, packaged food, beverages, discount retail, and household products.

Copper & Electrification Metals.description: Copper and related electrification metals needed for grids, energy transition, and industrial buildout.

Crypto & Digital Assets.description: Bitcoin miners, crypto exchanges, blockchain infrastructure, and BTC treasury proxies.

Defense & Rearming.description: Defense modernization, NATO spending, public-safety technology, and adjacent space/ISR exposure.

Digital Infrastructure REITs.description: REITs exposed to digital infrastructure demand, including data centers, colocation, and wireless tower networks.

Energy Sector.description: The full traditional Energy complex covered by Equipicker: integrated oils, upstream producers, midstream infrastructure, refiners, oilfield services, and uranium fuel exposure. For article purposes, the sector is presented through 6 editorial layers, with Drilling folded into Oilfield Services & Drilling for narrative simplicity.

Energy: Oil & Gas Integrated.description: The diversified supermajors with upstream, trading, refining, chemicals, and balance-sheet scale.
Energy: Oil & Gas Integrated.article_narrative: This is the anchor layer of Energy. Integrated majors are usually the cleanest starting point for retail investors because they combine commodity exposure with scale, dividends, downstream ballast, and capital-allocation flexibility. Many retail investors loosely discuss COP alongside the US majors, but operationally it remains upstream-only and is kept in E&P here.

Energy: Oil & Gas E&P.description: Independent upstream producers and royalty-style names with direct exposure to oil, gas, and production volumes.
Energy: Oil & Gas E&P.article_narrative: E&P is the purest listed expression of commodity exposure. Inside this layer, it helps to separate oil-weighted names, gas-weighted names, and royalty-linked models because the drivers, volatility, and valuation logic are not the same. For article use, the practical split is: oil-weighted = EOG, COP, FANG, DVN, OVV, MTDR, VTLE, PR, APA, OXY; gas-weighted = EQT, CTRA, EXE; royalty / mineral = TPL, STR, KRP, BSM, DMLP.

Energy: Oil & Gas Midstream.description: Pipelines, gathering and processing systems, storage, transport infrastructure, and LNG-linked assets moving hydrocarbons from field to market.
sub_baskets: Energy Midstream: Core Pipelines & Cash Flows
Energy: Oil & Gas Midstream.article_narrative: Midstream is the toll-road layer of Energy. Cash flows are usually steadier than upstream because volumes and contracts matter more than day-to-day oil prices. This broader layer contains pipelines, gathering, processing, transport, and storage, while the LNG export angle is separated from the core cash-flow pipeline complex through two distinct sub-layers.

Energy Midstream & LNG.description: The narrower LNG-export-focused slice of Energy midstream: direct export developers plus the gas-infrastructure names most relevant to feedgas flows and LNG optionality.
Energy Midstream & LNG.article_narrative: This sub-layer isolates the LNG export and gas-security angle inside Midstream. It should help readers distinguish the broader toll-road story from the narrower export-capacity and feedgas optionality story.

Energy Midstream: Core Pipelines & Cash.description: The broader fee-based midstream toll-road complex: pipelines, gathering, transport, and storage names whose investment case leans more on contracted volumes and distributable cash flow than on direct LNG export optionality.
Energy Midstream: Core Pipelines & Cash.article_narrative: This sub-layer captures the steadier cash-flow side of Midstream: the pipeline, gathering, transport, and storage names that investors often treat as the yield-and-volume backbone of the Energy sector.

Energy: Oil & Gas Refining & Marketing.description: Refiners converting crude into fuels and other refined products, with earnings driven by crack spreads and utilization.
Energy: Oil & Gas Refining & Marketing.article_narrative: Refining is not a direct oil-price bet. What matters more is the spread between crude input costs and product prices, which makes this layer cyclical, operational, and often misunderstood by generalist investors.

Energy: Oilfield Services & Drilling.description: The picks-and-shovels layer of Energy: oilfield service providers, equipment makers, completions specialists, and drilling contractors selling activity and capacity to producers.
Energy: Oilfield Services & Drilling.article_narrative: This layer exists to separate resource owners from activity suppliers. These companies do not usually own the hydrocarbons; they sell tools, services, rigs, and project execution to producers. Inside the basket, it helps to distinguish broader service and equipment names such as SLB, HAL, BKR, NOV, FTI, and LBRT from pure drilling contractors such as PTEN and NBR, which usually carry the highest operating leverage.

Energy: Uranium.description: Uranium and nuclear-fuel exposure sitting inside the Energy classification and linking the old energy system to the electrification era.
Energy: Uranium.article_narrative: Uranium is small relative to oil and gas in market-cap terms, but strategically important. It gives the Energy article a bridge into energy security, baseload electricity, and the power needs of an increasingly electrified economy.

High-Yield Energy Income.description: Energy names where the equity case leans heavily on distributions, fee-based cash flow, or royalty-style income exposure.
High-Yield Energy Income.article_narrative: For many retail investors, Energy is not only a commodity story but an income story. This overlay groups the most relevant yield vehicles inside Energy, combining fee-based midstream and MLP cash-flow models with royalty and mineral exposure.

Energy Security & Geopolitics.description: Cross-cutting names most exposed to allied fuel security, LNG exports, Western nuclear-fuel resilience, and geopolitically driven energy repricing.
Energy Security & Geopolitics.article_narrative: This overlay should stay selective. It is not meant to include every large oil company, but rather the names whose investment case is especially tied to energy security, allied supply chains, or geopolitical premium.

Energy Transition.description: Grid modernization, renewables, storage, charging, and electrification infrastructure.

EV & Autonomous Vehicles.description: Electric vehicle OEMs, charging infrastructure, and autonomy-adjacent suppliers.

eVTOL & Urban Air Mobility.description: Electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft and next-generation urban mobility concepts.

Fintech & Digital Payments.description: Payment networks, neobanks, BNPL, and digital finance platforms disrupting traditional banking workflows.

GLP-1 & Obesity Economy.description: GLP-1 drug makers and adjacent beneficiaries across monitoring, delivery, and obesity-linked workflows.

Gold & Precious Metals.description: Gold miners and royalty/streaming companies used as macro hedges and real-rate-sensitive plays.

Healthcare Innovation.description: MedTech, biotech platforms, precision medicine, surgical robotics, diagnostics, and clinical-data innovation.

Homebuilders & Housing.description: Residential construction, housing-sensitive suppliers, and home-improvement demand proxies.

Insurance Innovation.description: Insurtech disruptors and AI-enabled underwriting versus traditional insurers and brokers.

Nuclear Renaissance.description: Nuclear generation, fuel-cycle exposure, and SMR-linked beneficiaries driven by clean baseload demand.

Pet Economy.description: Pet food, pet-health, and pet-retail names benefiting from resilient consumer spend on companion animals.

Quantum Computing.description: Pure-play quantum hardware and software names with high narrative intensity and high execution risk.

Restaurant & QSR Growth.description: Fast-casual momentum, franchise scalers, and restaurant innovation stories.

Reshoring & Industrial Renaissance.description: CHIPS Act, IRA, and domestic manufacturing buildout beneficiaries across equipment, materials, and industrial systems.

Robotics & Automation.description: Industrial automation, warehouse robotics, machine vision, and physical automation beneficiaries.

Shipping & Logistics.description: Container shipping, freight logistics, and rail-linked supply-chain operators.

Space Economy.description: Launch providers, satellite operators, space infrastructure, and earth-observation platforms.

Streaming & Digital Entertainment.description: Streaming platforms, gaming, creator ecosystems, and digital-content distribution.

Tobacco & Vice.description: High-yield tobacco and vice-exposed cash-flow businesses.

Travel & Leisure Recovery.description: Travel demand normalization across OTAs, cruises, hotels, airlines, and leisure destinations.

_schema.ai_revenue_exposure: direct = >15% revenue from AI products/services | indirect = AI enhances existing products but is not the primary revenue driver | none = no meaningful AI revenue component
_schema.ai_disruption_risk: high = core business model threatened by AI within 3-5 years | medium = partial disruption likely, adaptation required | low = limited AI disruption exposure | none = business model AI-resilient or benefits from AI
